metadata.title: Exploring the impact of trip patterns on spatially aggregated crashes using floating vehicle trajectory data and graph Convolutional Networks

abstract: In recent years, increased attention has been given to understanding the spatial pattern of crashes in urban areas. Accurately capturing the spatial relationship between crash counts and variables requires extracting hidden information from multiple data sources. In this study, we propose a machine learning model to explore the spatial impact of activity patterns on spatially aggregated crash counts. Our paper introduces a two-step framework: (a) the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model, an unsupervised method for mining hidden activity patterns from floating vehicle trajectory data, and (b) the Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) model, which builds the spatial relationship between multi-source data. The data and hidden activity patterns were aggregated into 175 Traffic Analysis Zones (TAZs) in San Francisco using spatial partitioning. The GCN model provided higher prediction accuracy than commonly used machine learning algorithms that did not consider combined spatial relationships and those that only considered traditional vehicle counts data. Furthermore, we used attribution algorithms to obtain the respective weight scores of each factor. Our results reveal that daily vehicle kilometers traveled, road density, population density, commercial activity during weekends, and residential activity during morning peak hours on weekdays are factors associated with crashes.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Exploring the impact of trip patterns on spatially aggregated crashes using floating vehicle traject
paragraphs: In recent years, there has been a growing focus on the spatial patterns of urban crashes. The analysis of spatially-aggregated crash data serves as a vital tool for transportation agencies to monitor regional safety and incorporate road safety into their planning schemes (Huang et al., 2010). This analysis, aggregating crash data across various spatial and temporal scales, enables the identification of high-risk areas and their contributing factors. The resulting insights are invaluable in aiding policymakers, transportation planners, and road safety professionals in devising strategies to mitigate future crashes (Abdel-Aty et al., 2013).

Previous research into the spatial patterns of crashes utilized various spatially aggregated levels for analysis (Abdel-Aty et al., 2011), encompassing states (Noland, 2003), counties (Huang et al., 2010), census wards, and grid-based structures (Xu and Huang, 2015). These studies developed aggregate crash regression models to comprehend the factors contributing to crashes at disparate spatial levels. These models connect crashes to a variety of explanatory factors, including road network features, traffic volume and speed, and aggregated demographic and socioeconomic attributes (Quddus, 2008) (Zeng and Huang, 2014).

Understanding traffic exposures is crucial when modeling spatially aggregated crash data (Huang et al., 2010) (Pervaz et al., 2022) (Sagar and Stamatiadis, 2022) and assessing the impact or risk of traffic counts (Afolayan et al., 2022) (Bao et al., 2017). Previous studies have primarily relied on basic metrics, such as traffic volume, trip counts, distance traveled, travel duration, or average speed, to estimate traffic exposures (Afolayan et al., 2022) (Li et al., 2007). While metrics such as total number of trips can be estimated through travel surveys or trip generation models, they have limitations. Firstly, travel surveys are based on sample surveys and may only provide representative estimates for specific time periods and geographic areas, resulting in errors in accuracy and precision (El et al., 1999) (El et al., 2005). Secondly, the survey period may not capture unexpected events, such as large public events or weather disasters, that can affect trip volumes. Lastly, traditional measures of traffic exposure, such as traffic volume and road density, fail to consider the variability in individual travel behavior and activity patterns, which significantly impact a person's exposure to traffic risks (Thouez et al., 2005). Therefore, researchers require new data sources to supplement traditional measures of traffic exposure.

Recent advancements in transportation information technology have led to the availability of high-quality traffic and human activity data, such as taxi GPS, bicycle usage, mobile signals, and social media data. These data sources offer researchers valuable insights into travel patterns and their association with traffic crashes, presenting a new avenue for estimating traffic exposures. Various techniques exist for extracting activity patterns from multiple data sources, and these patterns can be utilized as variables when modeling spatially aggregated crash data (Xu et al., 2012) (Zhao et al., 2021). This approach allows transportation experts to enhance their understanding of the contributing factors and mechanisms underlying crashes. Individuals generate unique travel patterns as they engage in daily activities across different times and locations. These patterns can be classified or clustered to reveal distinct behaviors (Thouez et al., 2005) (Hanson and Hanson, 1981), incorporating factors like hours, minutes, days of the week or weekend, and types of land use. Kan et al. (Kan et al., 2022) analyzed how various activities, such as dining, shopping, work-related tasks, and recreation, expose individuals to traffic congestion. Elias et al. (Elias et al., 2010) assessed the impact of transportation and social policies on daily activity and travel patterns related to work, education, and other activities. In a previous study by Bao et al. (Bao et al., 2017), twitter check-in data was utilized to explore the relationship between crash counts and seven categories of activities, including work-related, food-related, entertainment-related, recreational-related, shopping-related, socialrelated, and education-related activities. Subsequently, in a study by Bao et al. (Bao et al., 2018), taxi trips with pick-up and drop-off points were employed to investigate how trip pattern variables affect crashes at various spatially aggregated levels.

Existing research has been primarily focused on trip patterns, predominantly steered by the trip purpose or the trip origin-destination concept, to comprehend their influence on crashes across various spatially aggregated levels. Additionally, it's critical to underscore that exclusive reliance on pick-up and drop-off data might not lead to a comprehensive understanding of trip dynamics. Focusing only on documenting origin and destination points may miss crucial aspects of the actual route chosen and the overall travel behaviour. To overcome these limitations, forthcoming studies need refined data sources such as trajectory data to gain a more nuanced understanding of trip activities and their correlation with traffic safety. In this paper, we suggest employing floating vehicle trajectory data to discover potential activity patterns in study area. These patterns are then incorporated into models of spatially aggregated crash data.

Previous studies have developed different spatial analysis models, such as the Bayesian Model (Huang and Abdel-Aty, 2010), Spatial Autoregressive Model (SAR), Spatial Error Model (SEM), Spatial Durbin Model (SDM) (Zhou et al., 2022), and Geographically Weighted Regression (Dong et al., 2020), to identify spatial patterns of crashes and investigate influential factors. These models have been employed to account for spatial heterogeneity, capture non-linear relationships, and handle spatial autocorrelation (Liu et al., 2017). However, these models often encounter limitations as they rely on various model assumptions, including linearity, normality, and independence. They may not fully capture the intricate and dynamic nature of traffic safety phenomena. In contrast, data-driven models are crafted to directly learn patterns and relationships from the observed data, without imposing stringent assumptions about the underlying data-generating process. Data-driven models can adaptively adjust algorithms and models based on the data, making them more fitting for intricate real-world scenarios in traffic safety analysis (Shankar et al., 1995) (Pan et al., 2017). Traffic safety analysis commonly employs data-driven models like Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machines (SVMs), K-nearest neighbors (KNNs), Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), and Clustering Analysis (Dogru and Subasi, 2018) (Li et al., 2008) (Silva et al., 2020). Recently, Deep Learning (DL) techniques, particularly Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), have been successfully integrated into traffic safety analysis, demonstrating exceptional accuracy and efficiency in handling extensive traffic data and addressing intricate traffic scene challenges.

GCNs outperform traditional DL models when it comes to processing non-Euclidean structured data. They are highly suitable for analyzing graph-structured data, including social networks, chemical molecules, and traffic networks. GCNs offer three key advantages over other datadriven methods in traffic safety analysis (Zhao, 2020). Firstly, they capture spatial relationships and complex dependencies among different areas in the traffic network using graph structures (Yao et al., 2021) (Yao et al., 2019), resulting in more accurate modeling and prediction. Secondly, GCNs can handle large-scale traffic data efficiently, eliminating the need for manual feature extraction and processing typically required by traditional machine learning models, which struggle with large datasets. Lastly, GCNs are interpretable and provide explanations regarding the contribution of each node in the model to the prediction of traffic safety. This paper aims to investigate the potential of GCNs in establishing complex spatial relationships between crash counts and external variables. It also seeks to explore the spatial impacts of potential activity patterns on traffic crashes using interpretable methods.
The contributions of this study can be summarized as follows:

(1) The study proposes a Latent Dirichlet Allocation algorithm to discover various activity patterns from floating vehicle trajectory data. By incorporating these activity patterns into the analysis, the researchers address issues related to low quality and high error rates in traditional traffic exposures. These activity patterns enable a better understanding of the degree to which vehicles and users are exposed to traffic risks.

(2) The study employs a Graph Convolutional Network model to explore how multiple factors impact the spatial patterns of traffic crashes. The GCN model is designed to operate on graph structures in spatial modeling, making it a powerful tool for analyzing aggregated crash data in terms of spatial safety. Compared to statistical and commonly used deep learning methods, the GCN model is particularly effective in this regard.

(3) Understanding the non-linear relationships between variables and traffic crashes is essential to developing effective strategies for improving road safety. This study leverages interpretive methods to explore these relationships and gain a more in-depth understanding of how multiple factors influence traffic crash spatial patterns. By doing so, the study aims to inform the development of more effective strategies for preventing crashes and improving road safety, which is a critical problem that demands immediate attention.

The rest paper is composed of five sections. The second section presents the process of data collection and preparation in detail. The third section provides an overview of the framework and methodology employed in this study. In the fourth section, the findings of the data analysis are illustrated. Finally, the last section summarizes the conclusions drawn from this research.

sections.paper_id: Zhao et al. - 2024 - Exploring the impact of trip patterns on spatially aggregated crashes using floating vehicle traject
paragraphs: This study used data from the City of San Francisco, USA, to integrate large-scale floating vehicle trajectory data into spatial analysis of crashes. San Francisco has been a leader in implementing open data policies, making a variety of data sources available to researchers, including real-time traffic flow data, land use data, census data, and crash data. The study covered the period from January to December 2020. Census blocks (CBs) are geographic areas defined by discernible features such as streets, roads, waterways, and railways, or by imperceptible boundaries like city, town, township, county limits, and brief line-of-sight extensions of streets and roads. The shapefile for CB boundaries was sourced from the United States Census Bureau's TIGER Files. The dataset included 175 traffic analysis zones (TAZs) located within San Francisco, with the exact boundaries of each TAZ shown in Fig. 1.

The researchers collected seven categories of data, which included crash data, traffic events data, floating vehicle trajectory data, crash exposure variables, road network characteristics, land use data, and socioeconomic information. The crash data was obtained from the US-Accidents: A Countrywide Traffic Accident Dataset, which provided information on variables such as the time, date, severity, weather conditions, and geographic location of each crash. During the study period, a total of 2,894 crashes were reported in the designated study area, and their spatial distribution across various TAZs is illustrated in Fig. 1.The traffic events data were obtained from the LSTW: Large-Scale Traffic and Weather Events Dataset. The LSTW was a large-scale, country-wide dataset for transportation and traffic research, which contained traffic and weather event data for the United States. The dataset had several types of events in terms of traffic, including congestion, construction, event (refers to the situations such as sports events, concerts, and demonstrations), broken-vehicle, flow accidents (refers to the problems such as broken traffic lights and animals in the road.), and lane-blocked. There were 76,458 traffic events in the study area during the selected time period.

The floating vehicle trajectory data used in this study were procured from the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency's computeraided dispatch and automated vehicle location system. This dataset contained relevant information, such as the reported location, date and time, vehicle identification, location coordinates in terms of latitude and longitude, heading, and speed. These trajectory data enabled the visualization of vehicle routes and the identification of traffic patterns across the city. More specially, the process of extracting the trip patterns from the floating vehicle trajectory data was performed in a series of sequential steps. The initial step involved the selection of location points that fell exclusively within the study area. Subsequently, a filtering procedure was employed to eliminate impractical floating vehicle routes, which were primarily attributed to the malfunctioning of vehicle meters. Note that a floating vehicle route was deemed impractical if it met any of the following two conditions: (a) the speed remained at zero for a continuous period of 15 min, or (b) the travel distance was zero for 15 min. Furthermore, trajectory data that contained only location date, time, location latitude, and longitude were considered for subsequent analyses, given the pivotal role played by timestamp information in exploring trip patterns. Approximately 2.36 % of the total observations were identified as unreasoning trajectory data and were removed from the analysis. Consequently, a final dataset containing 393,556,112 trajectory data points recorded during the selected study period was obtained.

Additionally, the transit vehicle kilometers traveled were calculated as an adjunct to the measurement of traffic exposure. In order to ascertain trip patterns from the floating vehicle trajectory data, a distinct methodology was employed. Firstly, we assumed that the vehicles traveled along unique road segments for a duration of 30 min, without any repetition. Subsequently, we mapped the geo-locations to a unique road identification (ID) and selected the road ID while also accumulating the corresponding road length that the vehicles passed through during the 30-minute duration. To supplement incomplete routes, the Dijkstra Algorithm (Dijkstra, 2022) was utilized.

The vehicle miles traveled (VMT) data were procured from the San Francisco County Transportation Authority. It is pertinent to note that the Transportation Authority employed the San Francisco Chained Activity Model Process (SF-CHAMP) to calculate VMT by private automobiles and taxis, for distinct land-use types. The SF-CHAMP estimates were derived based on several factors, such as observed behavior from the California Household Travel Survey, Census data concerning automobile ownership rates and county-to-county worker flows, and vehicle counts and transit boarding. Additionally, the travel behavior in SF-CHAMP was calibrated to achieve accuracy.

The road network data were acquired by utilizing the City's Geographic Information System (GIS) Base-map, with the support of ArcGIS shapefiles. The shapefiles provided comprehensive information about the road network attributes, such as road type, road length, intersection, traffic signal, parking sign, and stop sign. The road network is visually depicted in Fig. 1.

The land use data included land-use type and intensity, collected from the City and Commercial databases of San Francisco. Land use categories were as follows: Cultural Institutional Educational (CIE), Medical (MED), Management Information Professional Services (MIP), Retail Entertainment (RET), Production Distribution Repair Industrial (PDR), Residential (RES), and Hotels Visitor Services (VIS).

The social-demographic data utilized in this study were acquired from the U.S. Census Bureau, and encompassed information such as the number of individuals categorized by age, gender, and ethnicity. Subsequently, all the datasets were collated and integrated into corresponding TAZs, using ArcGIS 10.2. The descriptive statistics of the variables have been summarized in Table 1.

Census blocks (CBs) are geographic areas defined by discernible features such as streets, roads, waterways, and railways, or by imperceptible boundaries like city, town, township, county limits, and brief line-of-sight extensions of streets and roads. The shapefile for CB boundaries was sourced from the United States Census Bureau's TIGER Files. The dataset included 175 traffic analysis zones (TAZs) located within San Francisco, with the exact boundaries of each TAZ shown in Fig. 1.

The researchers collected seven categories of data, which included crash data, traffic events data, floating vehicle trajectory data, crash exposure variables, road network characteristics, land use data, and socioeconomic information. The crash data was obtained from the US-Accidents: A Countrywide Traffic Accident Dataset, which provided information on variables such as the time, date, severity, weather conditions, and geographic location of each crash. During the study period, a total of 2,894 crashes were reported in the designated study area, and their spatial distribution across various TAZs is illustrated in Fig. 1.The traffic events data were obtained from the LSTW: Large-Scale Traffic and Weather Events Dataset. The LSTW was a large-scale, country-wide dataset for transportation and traffic research, which contained traffic and weather event data for the United States. The dataset had several types of events in terms of traffic, including congestion, construction, event (refers to the situations such as sports events, concerts, and demonstrations), broken-vehicle, flow accidents (refers to the problems such as broken traffic lights and animals in the road.), and lane-blocked. There were 76,458 traffic events in the study area during the selected time period.

The floating vehicle trajectory data used in this study were procured from the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency's computeraided dispatch and automated vehicle location system. This dataset contained relevant information, such as the reported location, date and time, vehicle identification, location coordinates in terms of latitude and longitude, heading, and speed. These trajectory data enabled the visualization of vehicle routes and the identification of traffic patterns across the city. More specially, the process of extracting the trip patterns from the floating vehicle trajectory data was performed in a series of sequential steps. The initial step involved the selection of location points that fell exclusively within the study area. Subsequently, a filtering procedure was employed to eliminate impractical floating vehicle routes, which were primarily attributed to the malfunctioning of vehicle meters. Note that a floating vehicle route was deemed impractical if it met any of the following two conditions: (a) the speed remained at zero for a continuous period of 15 min, or (b) the travel distance was zero for 15 min. Furthermore, trajectory data that contained only location date, time, location latitude, and longitude were considered for subsequent analyses, given the pivotal role played by timestamp information in exploring trip patterns. Approximately 2.36 % of the total observations were identified as unreasoning trajectory data and were removed from the analysis. Consequently, a final dataset containing 393,556,112 trajectory data points recorded during the selected study period was obtained.

Additionally, the transit vehicle kilometers traveled were calculated as an adjunct to the measurement of traffic exposure. In order to ascertain trip patterns from the floating vehicle trajectory data, a distinct methodology was employed. Firstly, we assumed that the vehicles traveled along unique road segments for a duration of 30 min, without any repetition. Subsequently, we mapped the geo-locations to a unique road identification (ID) and selected the road ID while also accumulating the corresponding road length that the vehicles passed through during the 30-minute duration. To supplement incomplete routes, the Dijkstra Algorithm (Dijkstra, 2022) was utilized.

The vehicle miles traveled (VMT) data were procured from the San Francisco County Transportation Authority. It is pertinent to note that the Transportation Authority employed the San Francisco Chained Activity Model Process (SF-CHAMP) to calculate VMT by private automobiles and taxis, for distinct land-use types. The SF-CHAMP estimates were derived based on several factors, such as observed behavior from the California Household Travel Survey, Census data concerning automobile ownership rates and county-to-county worker flows, and vehicle counts and transit boarding. Additionally, the travel behavior in SF-CHAMP was calibrated to achieve accuracy.

The road network data were acquired by utilizing the City's Geographic Information System (GIS) Base-map, with the support of ArcGIS shapefiles. The shapefiles provided comprehensive information about the road network attributes, such as road type, road length, intersection, traffic signal, parking sign, and stop sign. The road network is visually depicted in Fig. 1.

The land use data included land-use type and intensity, collected from the City and Commercial databases of San Francisco. Land use categories were as follows: Cultural Institutional Educational (CIE), Medical (MED), Management Information Professional Services (MIP), Retail Entertainment (RET), Production Distribution Repair Industrial (PDR), Residential (RES), and Hotels Visitor Services (VIS).

The social-demographic data utilized in this study were acquired from the U.S. Census Bureau, and encompassed information such as the number of individuals categorized by age, gender, and ethnicity. Subsequently, all the datasets were collated and integrated into corresponding TAZs, using ArcGIS 10.2. The descriptive statistics of the variables have been summarized in Table 1.

These thematic structures are automatically identified based on the statistical properties of the documents, thereby negating the need for any prior labeling or annotation. Consequently, the identified thematic structures can be utilized to automatically categorize or summarize documents, in a manner that would be infeasible to achieve manually. The methodology adopted in this study was entirely data-driven, and thus capable of generating any hidden activity patterns that may be present within the data.

In a LDA model, each word and document are assumed to be generated according to the following process:Here, M represents the number of documents in the training corpus, K represents the number of topics,N represents the number of words in the vocabulary, θ is a M*K matrix, θ m ̅→ is the topic distribution for topic m. φ is a M*K matrix, φ k ̅→ is the distribution over the vocabulary. Let α and β be the prior parameters for the Dirichlet document-topic distribution and topic-word distribution, respectively. Dir( • ) is the Dirichlet distribution, and Multinomial( • ) is the multinomial distribution.

In LDA, the fundamental unit is a "word," and all inputs are categorical variables. As such, each activity record must be converted into labels for input into the LDA model. l. To achieve this, a crucial feature extraction process was first applied to the floating vehicle trajectories, aimed at comprehensively characterizing the activity patterns. This feature extraction process encompassed variables such as the week name, hour, and land use type. Subsequently, these key features were grouped together and transformed into an activity label (see Fig. 2). The land-use features were simplified into five land-use types for floating vehicle travel activities and assigned to floating vehicle geo-locations. More specifically, the geo-locations of floating vehicles were assigned with the corresponding census tracts. For example, a floating vehicle #n provided a trajectory from 9:00 to 9:30. It is worth noting that a time unit of 30 min was deemed appropriate for this study. Map these trajectories to road segments and land use types. There were five trajectory records between 9:00 and 9:30, including four trajectory records about Wed. 9 Com. and one trajectory record about Wed. 9 Res. Highfrequency records can represent activity characteristics. Therefore, this record was transformed into an activity label as Wed. 9 Com. in this study.

In this study, the activity labels, as depicted in Fig. 3, served as the fundamental units of analysis for exploring activity patterns using the LDA method. Each label was considered as a word of a topic, and each activity was treated as a topic of a document. The trajectory records in each transportation analysis zone (TAZ) were regarded as the documents of a corpus. Furthermore, the total trip records in the entire dataset were allocated to each TAZ based on their origins, following the definition of activity labels.

Let us assume that K denotes the number of trip patterns, M denotes the number of TAZs, N denotes the number of trip labels for each trip pattern, θ denotes the mixture weights for the N trip patterns in each TAZ; and φ denotes the mixture weights for the trip labels in each trip pattern. The latent trip patterns can be depicted via the generating mechanism of the LDA method, as illustrated in Fig. 4.

The LDA estimation can be implemented using several algorithms such as the EM algorithm, E expectation-Propagation algorithm, and Gibbs Sampling. Among these algorithms, Gibbs Sampling is the most commonly used inference method for LDA, owing to its simplicity, directness, and fast convergence. So this paper used Gibbs Sampling to estimate LDA. The specific derivation process can be shown in these papers (Darling, 2011) (Porteous et al., 2008). The smaller the degree of Perplexity is, the stronger the generation ability of the LDA model is.

Röder (Röder et al., 2015) devised a set of coherence metrics by employing a novel method. Specifically, the method involved creating content vectors of words by taking their co-occurrences into account. The coherence score was subsequently calculated using the normalized point-wise mutual information (NPMI) and the cosine similarity. The approach entailed four distinct steps: (a) segmentation of the data into word pairs, (b) calculation of word or word pair probabilities, (c) computation of a confirmation measure that gauges the degree of support that one word set lends to another, and (d) integration of individual confirmation measures into an overarching coherence score. Here, this paper uses coherence as our choice of metric for performance comparison.

The present study utilized both the perplexity and coherence methods to identify the most suitable number of topics. The optimal number of topics was determined by comparing the outcomes obtained through these two methods.

paragraphs: Graph Convolutional Neural network is one of the important fields of deep learning method, which usually focuses on how to aggregate information from the perspective of spatial structure data. In essence, a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) can be conceptualized as employing the underlying graph structure as the computation graph for message propagation. During this process, the representations of the neighboring nodes are aggregated to obtain a more contextualized representation that is spatially aware. The GCN model provides a better way to mine the correlation between land use, road network, traffic flow, trip pattern and accident by combining the multi-source data in the city.

A graph G = (V , E ) is a structure used to represent entities and their relationship, as shown in Fig. 5. It consists of two sets -the set of nodes V (also called vertices) and the set of edges E (also called arcs). An edge ( v i , v j ) ∈ E connecting a pair of nodes v i and v j indicates that there is a relation between them. An adjacency matrix A ∈ R N×N comprises binary values of 0 and 1. The value 0 signifies the absence of a link between a pair of nodes, whereas the value 1 indicates the presence of a link. In the paper, each node contains traffic related variables, road related variables, land use-related variables, demographic related variables and activity pattern variables. The adjacency matrix shows the spatial connectivity of each TAZ.

The GCN model operates on an input consisting of an adjacency matrix, denoted by x, and a node feature matrix, denoted by y. The GCN model employs a Fourier domain filter to capture spatial features between nodes in the graph, based on their first-order neighborhood. By stacking multiple convolutional layers, the GCN model can be constructed, which operates on the nodes of the graph and leverages the filter to capture spatial information. This approach is particularly useful for analyzing graph-structured data and has demonstrated success in a variety of applications. Formally, a specific GCN model f(X, A) is given, with the following form of a layer-wise propagation rule provided by Kipf and Welling (Kipf and Welling, 2016):

where Ã = A +I N is the adjacency matrix with the addition of selfconnection. I N is the identity matrix. Dii = ∑ j Ãij is denoted as degree matrix. W (l) is a layer-specific weight matrix determined by the training. σ(•) is a nonlinear activation function such as 'ReLU'.
The loss function of GCN is defined as:

Here, L 0 demotes the supervised loss w.r.t. the labeled part of the graph, λ is a weighing factor and X is a matrix of node feature vectors. f( • ) can be a neural network like differentiable function. Specifically, our findings indicate that there are comparable patterns in the distribution of these two datasets across space and time. The finding also could be shown in these three figures. It is a strong spatiotemporal relationship between floating vehicle trips and crashes. However, the relationship is complex and dynamic. It is inappropriate to use the number of floating vehicle trips as a variable in the crashes model. We also need some external efforts to extract the information from the floating vehicle closely related to crashes. Fig. 8. Perplexity and Coherence versus the number of activity patterns.

paragraphs: Prior to specifying a LDA model, it is necessary to pre-specify the hyper-parameters of α, β, and the number of floating vehicle activity patterns (K). Usually, the most commonly applied procedure is α = 50 K , and β = 0.1 (Wang et al., 2016). The activity patterns are intuitively interpretable and are evaluated based on coherence and perplexity measures, which assess the fitness of the LDA model to the floating vehicle dataset. Specifically, lower perplexity scores are indicative of better performance by the LDA model, while higher coherence scores correspond to better performance.
In this study, when K = 8, the score of the perplexity and coherence get better results. Fig. 8 shows the number of activity patterns identified with the LDA model.

To define a specific activity pattern, the top ten activity labels are identified based on their corresponding probability values. The examination of these activity labels facilitates the extraction of important information regarding the day of the week, the time of day, and associated land use characteristics of each floating vehicle trip. Fig. 9 presents the eight activity patterns that were chosen based on the insights obtained from the activity labels. The characteristics of these eight activity patterns can be described as follows:
Activity during weekends at midnight, reflecting a leisurely and relaxed lifestyle during their rest time.

Activity Pattern 8: Midnight Residential Activity Pattern. It signifies residential activity during midnight. Individuals are active in residential and mixed areas, possibly indicating people returning home after working overtime. This group of people is active from 9 pm to the early morning hours.

This paper conducts a comparative analysis of popular post-hoc saliency methods that are based on backpropagation and perturbation approaches. These methods offer estimations of feature importance or relevance for each input feature at a particular time step. In this benchmark, the following saliency methods are included:

Integrated Gradients (Sundararajan et al., 2017) calculate the average gradient with respect to changes in input values from a noninformative reference point. It defines the attribute of the feature i as: the path integral of linear path from baseline x '
i to input x i :
where a function G : R n →[0, 1] represents a neural network, ∂G(x) ∂x i is the gradient of G(x) along the dimension i th .

Smoothing noisy gradients (Smilkov et al., 2017) involves computing gradients n th times while adding noise to the input on each iteration. This method relies on two hyperparameters: the standard deviation of the Gaussian perturbations, denoted by σ, and the number of samples to average over, denoted by n. Specifically, the approach entails generating random samples in a neighborhood of an input x and averaging the resulting sensitivity graphs.

In the defined study area, there are a total of 175 TAZs. Of these, 31 TAZs are categorized as zero-crash analysis units, constituting approximately 17.71 % of the overall count. The GCN model does not assume specific data distribution parameters. This is particularly advantageous when confronting zero-inflated datasets, especially considering the marked disparities in crash rates among various TAZs (Lord et al., 2007;Yu et al., 2021). The GCN mitigates the effects of data skewness by employing suitable loss functions and weight modifications, making it adept at predicting minority class events (Wang et al., 2021). Consequently, our models can handle the data including zero-crash TAZs. The datasets were split into training, validation, and test sets at a ratio of 6:2:2. Prior to analysis, all data was normalized using the Min-Max method, resulting in a range of values between -1 and 1. The tested model is deployed with the same following hyper-parameters to compare performance under the same conditions. a) The number of the convolutional layer in the hidden layer is 2, respectively; b) The Activation function is 'ReLU'; c) L2-based MSE is used for the loss function; d) Optimizer Adam (Adaptive moment estimation), a commonly used training algorithm (Kingma and Ba, 2014), is used to improve training efficiency; and e) 5-fold cross-validation is used in training to avoid overfitting.

The loss function error determines the proposed architecture's best parameters. When the epoch is 500 and the learning rate is 0.01, the performance is the best: 5.63. Fig. 10 depicts the difference between the predicted value and the real value. The red color generally indicates a high error. The TAZ #116 and TAZ #33 have the big differences between the predicted value and the real value of the red areas. There is a Dwight D. Eisenhower Hwy in the TAZ #116 and a Southern Fwy in the TAZ #33. More crashes would occur along freeways. In addition, the highways play a fast and efficient travel function in urban traffic, which is quite different from the functionality of urban internal roads. So it is not applicable for us to identify highway traffic crashes with daily activity patterns.

This paper compares the results with other machine learning models, such as the Tree model, AdaBoost model, Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Deep Neural Networks (DNN). The hyperparameters of the models are shown in Table 2. The prediction error of MSE, MAE, and RMSE are shown in Table 3. The GCN model demonstrates superior performance with an MSE of 5.632, an MAE of 6.143, and an RMSE of 0.574. The model excels at reducing both minor and significant prediction errors, as evident from its notably low MSE and MAE values. The RMSE values are not the lowest among the models. This is because the RMSE metric, by squaring the differences between predicted and actual values, gives more weight to larger errors caused by outliers. Due to the GCN's nature of smooth results in spatial dimension, it exhibits reduced predictive accuracy for anomalous samples, resulting in large errors in anomalous samples. In summary, considering the performance across MSE, MAE, and RMSE, our GCN model outperforms all other models in this study.

This paper additionally explores the impact of floating vehicle counts on the model's results. Our GCN model evaluates the performance of two types of input data, namely, activity patterns and floating vehicle counts. The comparison, as detailed in Table 4, indicates that the GCN model based on activity patterns yields a MSE of 5.632, a MAE of 6.143, and a RMSE of 0.574. Conversely, the GCN model grounded in floating vehicle counts demonstrates slightly inferior performance with a MSE of 6.582, a MAE of 6.402, and a RMSE of 0.623.

This disparity in performance reiterates the efficacy of our approach within the GCN model. It showcases superior accuracy in predicting crash occurrences when leveraging activity patterns rather than floating vehicle counts. This could be attributed to the premise that specific activity patterns might encapsulate more relevant information associated with crash incidences. In essence, certain activity patterns could be linked with a higher propensity for crashes at specific times or locales. By segregating these activity patterns, the model potentially enhances its precision in predicting crash occurrences.

This paper adopts interpretive models to analyze the GCN model and discern crucial features contributing to crash count predictions. The correlation coefficients between crash counts and each independent variable are illustrated in Fig. 11. The findings can be summarized as follows:

Influential Factors: The analysis reveals that several factors exhibit significant correlations with crash counts. These include broken-vehicle density, DVKT, density of all roads, density of avenues, and activity patterns. Notably, increases in these factors are associated with higher crash counts. These results highlight the importance of considering these variables when predicting crash occurrences.

Negative Correlations: Conversely, certain variables demonstrate negative correlations with crash counts. These variables encompass speed limits below 15 mph and 35 mph, intersection density, stop sign density, residential (RES) density, population density, and age distribution above 50. Lower values of these indices are linked to a reduced likelihood of crashes. These findings underscore the potential Insignificant Effects: The analysis indicates that congestion density, MIPS area, RES area, PDR area, and age distribution below 20 exhibit no noticeable impact on crash counts. These variables, in this context, do not play a significant role in predicting crash occurrences.

Other attribution algorithms have also identified the importance of these features, thus enabling comparisons. Fig. 12 facilitates an understanding of the similarities or differences in attribution scores assigned by distinct algorithms. Moreover, the comparison of attribution scores with learned model weights provides insight into the relationships between the dependent variable (crash counts) and each independent variable. A weight of zero signifies no correlation, while positive and negative weights imply positive and negative correlations, respectively.

In this study, attributions are computed utilizing a variety of attribution algorithms, and the outcomes are visualized. Standard settings, such as default baselines and the number of steps, are consistently applied across all algorithms.

The attribution algorithms employed in this study occasionally demonstrate discrepancies in assigning importance scores and do not always correspond with learned model weights. However, consistent identification of certain features, such as DVKT, the density of all roads, the density of avenues, the density of highways, population density and activity patterns 2 and 4, underscores their importance across the majority of attribution algorithms and learned coefficients. Additionally, these important variables in zone-level crash models include DVKT, road density, highway density, and population density. This observation aligns intuitively with findings from multiple previous studies (Huang Fig. 11. The correlation coefficient between crash counts and each independent variable. et al., 2010) (Zhang et al., 2023) (Li et al., 2013) (Ladrón De Guevara et al., 2004). Chakravarthy et al.'s study revealed an increasing crash frequency in specific regions recognized as commercial or recreational places (Chakravarthy et al., 2010). Furthermore, data from Lightstone et al. (Lightstone et al., 2001) indicated that a majority of pedestrian injuries in crashes transpire in close proximity to their residences.

Population density interestingly receives a high negative attribution score according to some attribution algorithms. This result may be subject to the selection of the baseline, set to zero for all features in this study, suggesting that more carefully chosen baselines could significantly alter attribution scores. An intriguing observation from the study is the consistent similarity in attribution scores between Integrated Gradients and DeepLift across all features, possibly due to the minimal impact of non-linearities in the model, causing DeepLift to closely resemble the (input -baselines) × gradients (Sturmfels et al., 2020). Additionally, the gradients show no significant variations along the straight line connecting the baseline and input, leading to a similar scenario with Integrated Gradients.

The study reveals that activity pattern 2, representing commercial activities during weekends, has a direct impact on traffic safety. The high concentration of floating vehicles in commercial districts, such as districts 3, 5, and 6, may escalate traffic congestion and increase the likelihood of accidents during peak hours. The popularity of weekends for tourists visiting these areas could further intensify traffic and pedestrian activity. According to the 2020 national household travel survey by the Federal Highway Administration, Marshall W. Meyer discovered shopping and socializing trends (Meyer, 2020), substantially increasing the potential crash risk.

Similarly, activity pattern 4, embodying residential activities during weekday morning peak hours, may have implications for traffic safety. Increased traffic volume and congestion on the roads due to commuting residents could heighten the risk of accidents. Furthermore, limited transportation options in residential areas could result in greater dependence on personal vehicles, escalating the probability of accidents. Furthermore, in these studies conducted by Bao et al. (Bao et al., 2018) and Ponicki et al. (Ponicki et al., 2013); they was also observed that commuting journeys typically comprise a substantial portion of the overall daily trip volume, thereby exerting significant influence on the incidence of crashes.

Moreover, this paper pays more attention to the traffic exposures of the safety analysis. Fig. 13 presents a summary of the percentage of all floating vehicles in each supervisorial district, providing insights into how overall floating vehicle usage varies by time of day and location. Our estimates indicate that in districts 3 and 6, more than 40 % of floating vehicles are concentrated in San Francisco's northeast quadrant, including South of Market, the Mission Street corridor, the Van Ness Avenue corridor, Pacific Heights, and the Marina, all of which exhibit relatively higher levels of vehicle usage. Referring to the San Francisco Taxi Stand Locations dataset provided by the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (https://www.sfmta.com/maps/san-francisco-t axi-stand-locations), regulations in districts 3 and 6 enhance the ease of hailing taxis at street level. This circumstance also contributes to the regular presence of floating vehicles within these districts. Figure 15 also shows the weights for each activity pattern by the supervisorial district. District 6 is characterized as the most congested area in the city, despite being well served by public transit, bicycling, and walking facilities. Our analysis shows that Activity Patterns 3 and 6 in District 6 have higher weights than other districts and are primarily associated with work activities. This is likely due to the significant employment opportunities and public transit hubs found in the district, as well as higher parking supply restrictions and costs. This assertion is further supported by San Francisco's adoption of Demand-Responsive Parking Pricing. Parking pricing in District 6 surpasses $6 per hour and escalates to $8 per hour during the dual peak timeframe (https://www.sfmta.com/demand-res ponsive-parking-pricing).

There are some special places for the traffic safety analysis, including the golden gate park in the districts 1, the Presidio of San Francisco in the district 2 and the Bay Shore Freeway in the district 9. An example of hazardous driving conditions can be observed in the Golden Gate Park, where surface water on the road can pose a risk to drivers. In response to such issues, the Golden Gate Park Access and Safety Program has implemented measures to reduce traffic crashes, such as enforcing speed limits. Additionally, we observe that the concentration of floating vehicle usage in these areas is particularly high on Saturdays, along with a higher number of trips originating from Golden Gate Park. This is consistent with activity pattern 2.The Presidio of San Francisco in the district 2 has a low weight about the work activity about activity pattern 3 and activity pattern 6. The study conducted by LaScala et al. (LaScala et al., 2000) also emphasized that particular TAZs exhibited notably unique characteristics relevant to traffic safety analysis. Instances of such distinctive attributes comprise the Presidio base, where substantial traffic flow directly to and from the Golden Gate Bridge is coupled with a low road density and high crash risk. Additionally, Golden Gate Park is characterized by significant vehicular and pedestrian movement despite its modest population density.

The activity pattern 2 and activity pattern 7 have higher weights in the commercial districts, like the districts 3, districts 5 and districts 6. The activity pattern 1, activity pattern 4, activity pattern 5, and activity pattern 8 might have an important weight in these resident areas, like districts 3, districts 5 and districts 6. Our analysis suggests that southeastern and southern neighborhoods of the city do not appear to be as well served by floating vehicles. This may reflect a lack of available vehicles in these areas or an inability of residents to use floating vehicles, or it could be attributed to a combination of these or other factors. This observation is supported by data from CalEnviroScreen (https:// oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen) and Transportation Authority (https ://epc-map.sfcta.org/), which indicates that southeastern and southern neighborhoods exhibit elevated proportions of households with minority or low-income status, senior residents, individuals with limited English proficiency, individuals with disabilities, and other related factors.

Furthermore, the concentration of floating vehicles in certain areas and during certain activity patterns can also impact traffic safety through factors such as air pollution and noise levels (Maizlish et al., 2013). Activity pattern 2 may lead to increased emissions from idling vehicles (Rabl and De Nazelle, 2012). Activity pattern 1 and activity pattern 4 may result in increased noise levels during morning and afternoon rush hour. It is important for city planners and policymakers to consider these factors when making decisions about transportation infrastructure and policies. This may involve implementing measures such as traffic calming measures, promoting public transportation and active transportation options, and adjusting traffic signals and signage to improve traffic flow and reduce congestion.

The model excels at reducing both minor and significant prediction errors, as evident from its notably low MSE and MAE values. The RMSE values are not the lowest among the models. This is because the RMSE metric, by squaring the differences between predicted and actual values, gives more weight to larger errors caused by outliers. Due to the GCN's nature of smooth results in spatial dimension, it exhibits reduced predictive accuracy for anomalous samples, resulting in large errors in anomalous samples. In summary, considering the performance across MSE, MAE, and RMSE, our GCN model outperforms all other models in this study.

This paper additionally explores the impact of floating vehicle counts on the model's results. Our GCN model evaluates the performance of two types of input data, namely, activity patterns and floating vehicle counts. The comparison, as detailed in Table 4, indicates that the GCN model based on activity patterns yields a MSE of 5.632, a MAE of 6.143, and a RMSE of 0.574. Conversely, the GCN model grounded in floating vehicle counts demonstrates slightly inferior performance with a MSE of 6.582, a MAE of 6.402, and a RMSE of 0.623.

This disparity in performance reiterates the efficacy of our approach within the GCN model. It showcases superior accuracy in predicting crash occurrences when leveraging activity patterns rather than floating vehicle counts. This could be attributed to the premise that specific activity patterns might encapsulate more relevant information associated with crash incidences. In essence, certain activity patterns could be linked with a higher propensity for crashes at specific times or locales. By segregating these activity patterns, the model potentially enhances its precision in predicting crash occurrences.

This paper adopts interpretive models to analyze the GCN model and discern crucial features contributing to crash count predictions. The correlation coefficients between crash counts and each independent variable are illustrated in Fig. 11. The findings can be summarized as follows:

Influential Factors: The analysis reveals that several factors exhibit significant correlations with crash counts. These include broken-vehicle density, DVKT, density of all roads, density of avenues, and activity patterns. Notably, increases in these factors are associated with higher crash counts. These results highlight the importance of considering these variables when predicting crash occurrences.

Negative Correlations: Conversely, certain variables demonstrate negative correlations with crash counts. These variables encompass speed limits below 15 mph and 35 mph, intersection density, stop sign density, residential (RES) density, population density, and age distribution above 50. Lower values of these indices are linked to a reduced likelihood of crashes. These findings underscore the potential Insignificant Effects: The analysis indicates that congestion density, MIPS area, RES area, PDR area, and age distribution below 20 exhibit no noticeable impact on crash counts. These variables, in this context, do not play a significant role in predicting crash occurrences.

Other attribution algorithms have also identified the importance of these features, thus enabling comparisons. Fig. 12 facilitates an understanding of the similarities or differences in attribution scores assigned by distinct algorithms. Moreover, the comparison of attribution scores with learned model weights provides insight into the relationships between the dependent variable (crash counts) and each independent variable. A weight of zero signifies no correlation, while positive and negative weights imply positive and negative correlations, respectively.

In this study, attributions are computed utilizing a variety of attribution algorithms, and the outcomes are visualized. Standard settings, such as default baselines and the number of steps, are consistently applied across all algorithms.

The attribution algorithms employed in this study occasionally demonstrate discrepancies in assigning importance scores and do not always correspond with learned model weights. However, consistent identification of certain features, such as DVKT, the density of all roads, the density of avenues, the density of highways, population density and activity patterns 2 and 4, underscores their importance across the majority of attribution algorithms and learned coefficients. Additionally, these important variables in zone-level crash models include DVKT, road density, highway density, and population density. This observation aligns intuitively with findings from multiple previous studies (Huang Fig. 11. The correlation coefficient between crash counts and each independent variable. et al., 2010) (Zhang et al., 2023) (Li et al., 2013) (Ladrón De Guevara et al., 2004). Chakravarthy et al.'s study revealed an increasing crash frequency in specific regions recognized as commercial or recreational places (Chakravarthy et al., 2010). Furthermore, data from Lightstone et al. (Lightstone et al., 2001) indicated that a majority of pedestrian injuries in crashes transpire in close proximity to their residences.

Population density interestingly receives a high negative attribution score according to some attribution algorithms. This result may be subject to the selection of the baseline, set to zero for all features in this study, suggesting that more carefully chosen baselines could significantly alter attribution scores. An intriguing observation from the study is the consistent similarity in attribution scores between Integrated Gradients and DeepLift across all features, possibly due to the minimal impact of non-linearities in the model, causing DeepLift to closely resemble the (input -baselines) × gradients (Sturmfels et al., 2020). Additionally, the gradients show no significant variations along the straight line connecting the baseline and input, leading to a similar scenario with Integrated Gradients.

The study reveals that activity pattern 2, representing commercial activities during weekends, has a direct impact on traffic safety. The high concentration of floating vehicles in commercial districts, such as districts 3, 5, and 6, may escalate traffic congestion and increase the likelihood of accidents during peak hours. The popularity of weekends for tourists visiting these areas could further intensify traffic and pedestrian activity. According to the 2020 national household travel survey by the Federal Highway Administration, Marshall W. Meyer discovered shopping and socializing trends (Meyer, 2020), substantially increasing the potential crash risk.

Similarly, activity pattern 4, embodying residential activities during weekday morning peak hours, may have implications for traffic safety. Increased traffic volume and congestion on the roads due to commuting residents could heighten the risk of accidents. Furthermore, limited transportation options in residential areas could result in greater dependence on personal vehicles, escalating the probability of accidents. Furthermore, in these studies conducted by Bao et al. (Bao et al., 2018) and Ponicki et al. (Ponicki et al., 2013); they was also observed that commuting journeys typically comprise a substantial portion of the overall daily trip volume, thereby exerting significant influence on the incidence of crashes.

Moreover, this paper pays more attention to the traffic exposures of the safety analysis. Fig. 13 presents a summary of the percentage of all floating vehicles in each supervisorial district, providing insights into how overall floating vehicle usage varies by time of day and location. Our estimates indicate that in districts 3 and 6, more than 40 % of floating vehicles are concentrated in San Francisco's northeast quadrant, including South of Market, the Mission Street corridor, the Van Ness Avenue corridor, Pacific Heights, and the Marina, all of which exhibit relatively higher levels of vehicle usage. Referring to the San Francisco Taxi Stand Locations dataset provided by the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (https://www.sfmta.com/maps/san-francisco-t axi-stand-locations), regulations in districts 3 and 6 enhance the ease of hailing taxis at street level. This circumstance also contributes to the regular presence of floating vehicles within these districts. Figure 15 also shows the weights for each activity pattern by the supervisorial district. District 6 is characterized as the most congested area in the city, despite being well served by public transit, bicycling, and walking facilities. Our analysis shows that Activity Patterns 3 and 6 in District 6 have higher weights than other districts and are primarily associated with work activities. This is likely due to the significant employment opportunities and public transit hubs found in the district, as well as higher parking supply restrictions and costs. This assertion is further supported by San Francisco's adoption of Demand-Responsive Parking Pricing. Parking pricing in District 6 surpasses $6 per hour and escalates to $8 per hour during the dual peak timeframe (https://www.sfmta.com/demand-res ponsive-parking-pricing).

There are some special places for the traffic safety analysis, including the golden gate park in the districts 1, the Presidio of San Francisco in the district 2 and the Bay Shore Freeway in the district 9. An example of hazardous driving conditions can be observed in the Golden Gate Park, where surface water on the road can pose a risk to drivers. In response to such issues, the Golden Gate Park Access and Safety Program has implemented measures to reduce traffic crashes, such as enforcing speed limits. Additionally, we observe that the concentration of floating vehicle usage in these areas is particularly high on Saturdays, along with a higher number of trips originating from Golden Gate Park. This is consistent with activity pattern 2.The Presidio of San Francisco in the district 2 has a low weight about the work activity about activity pattern 3 and activity pattern 6. The study conducted by LaScala et al. (LaScala et al., 2000) also emphasized that particular TAZs exhibited notably unique characteristics relevant to traffic safety analysis. Instances of such distinctive attributes comprise the Presidio base, where substantial traffic flow directly to and from the Golden Gate Bridge is coupled with a low road density and high crash risk. Additionally, Golden Gate Park is characterized by significant vehicular and pedestrian movement despite its modest population density.

The activity pattern 2 and activity pattern 7 have higher weights in the commercial districts, like the districts 3, districts 5 and districts 6. The activity pattern 1, activity pattern 4, activity pattern 5, and activity pattern 8 might have an important weight in these resident areas, like districts 3, districts 5 and districts 6. Our analysis suggests that southeastern and southern neighborhoods of the city do not appear to be as well served by floating vehicles. This may reflect a lack of available vehicles in these areas or an inability of residents to use floating vehicles, or it could be attributed to a combination of these or other factors. This observation is supported by data from CalEnviroScreen (https:// oehha.ca.gov/calenviroscreen) and Transportation Authority (https ://epc-map.sfcta.org/), which indicates that southeastern and southern neighborhoods exhibit elevated proportions of households with minority or low-income status, senior residents, individuals with limited English proficiency, individuals with disabilities, and other related factors.

Furthermore, the concentration of floating vehicles in certain areas and during certain activity patterns can also impact traffic safety through factors such as air pollution and noise levels (Maizlish et al., 2013). Activity pattern 2 may lead to increased emissions from idling vehicles (Rabl and De Nazelle, 2012). Activity pattern 1 and activity pattern 4 may result in increased noise levels during morning and afternoon rush hour. It is important for city planners and policymakers to consider these factors when making decisions about transportation infrastructure and policies. This may involve implementing measures such as traffic calming measures, promoting public transportation and active transportation options, and adjusting traffic signals and signage to improve traffic flow and reduce congestion.

paragraphs: This paper proposed a framework for exploring how activity pattern variables extracted from floating vehicle trajectory data contribute to crash counts at a spatially aggregated level in urban areas. To accomplish this, we incorporated floating vehicle trajectory data to enhance traffic exposures and utilized a data-driven approach employing the unsupervised Latent Dirichlet Allocation model to uncover latent activity patterns. Subsequently, we constructed a Graph Convolutional Neural Network to establish the spatial relationship between crash counts and contributing factors. The results demonstrated that the GCN model performs well compared to other machine learning models, such as the Tree model, AdaBoost model, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, and Deep Neural Networks. Furthermore, comparative analyses were conducted to evaluate the performance of the GCN model using both traditional vehicle count data and activity patterns. The results demonstrated that activity patterns derived from the GCN model exhibited better performance. The results from interpretable models indicated that factors such as DVKT, broken-vehicle density, all road density, and activity patterns were positively associated with increased crash counts. Speed limits (less than 15 mph, 25-35 mph), intersection density, stop sign density, RES density, population density, and age distribution (over 50) exhibited a negative correlation with crash counts. Additionally, our analysis consistently identified DVKT, road density, population density, activity pattern 2 (Weekend Commercial Activity Pattern: commercial activity during weekends), and activity pattern 4 (Weekday Morning Residential Activity Pattern: residential activity during morning peak hours on weekdays) as significant factors based on both attribution algorithms and learned model weights.

The model performance can be enhanced by considering the following aspects. Firstly, despite the Mediterranean climate of the Bay Area, our extensive analysis indicates that seasonal variations strongly influence vehicle usage. Consequently, training the model using seasonal floating vehicle trajectory data may yield more robust and dependable findings in further analyses. Secondly, multi-source data exhibit intricate spatial relationships. One approach to addressing this is by constructing a heterogeneous graph to capture the spatial dependencies. Additionally, the integration of additional datasets, such as Twitter data, cell phone data, and ridership data, has the potential to provide further insights into the variability of crash counts at the zone level.
